11: Elisha’s weeping as he gazes at Hazael — seeing what Hazael will do to Israel — combines prophetic knowledge of coming judgment with the prophet’s grief over it. The man of God who announces judgment is not unmoved by it; the commission to speak the word of YHWH does not insulate the prophet from the weight of what that word contains. Luke 19:41-44 narrates Jesus weeping over Jerusalem: kai hōs ēggisen, idōn tēn polin eklausen ep’ autēn, ‘and when he drew near and saw the city, he wept over it, saying, “Would that you, even you, had known on this day the things that make for peace!”’ — and proceeds to announce the city’s destruction. The weeping prophet who announces judgment is the OT type that Jesus inhabits and fulfills in its deepest form: the one who is the judgment of God (John 5:22: ‘the Father judges no one but has given all judgment to the Son’) is also the one who weeps over the judgment falling on those who reject it. Elisha weeps because he foresees what Hazael will do; Jesus weeps because he foresees what Rome will do — and in both cases the weeping is the prophet’s authentic grief, not performance. Jeremiah 8:21-9:1 (‘for the wound of the daughter of my people is my heart wounded; is there no balm in Gilead?’) is the same prophetic structure, connecting Elisha, Jeremiah, and Jesus in a single pattern of judgment-announcing grief.
1: Elisha warns the Shunammite woman (cf. 2 Kings 4:8-37) that YHWH has called for a seven-year famine (ûgūr baʾăšer tāgûrî kî qārāʾ YHWH lārāʿāḇ wᵉgam bāʾ ʾel hāʾāreṣ šeḇaʿ šānîm) — leave and sojourn wherever you can. The Elisha-Shunammite narrative from 2 Kings 4 (resurrection of her son) is resumed here with the prophet's care for her material welfare. The seven-year famine matches the pattern of Gen 41 (Joseph's seven-year famine) — Elisha's prophetic forewarning enables the Shunammite to avoid the curse before it arrives, just as Joseph's interpretation enabled Egypt to prepare.
2: The woman acts on Elisha's word: wattāqom hāʾiššāh wattaʿaś kidbar ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm — she arose and did according to the man of God's word. She leaves for Philistia with her household and sojourns there seven years. Obedience to prophetic word preserves her through the famine. The contrast with unbelieving characters throughout the Elisha cycle is pointed: those who act on prophetic word receive blessing; those who doubt receive nothing or worse.
3: At the end of seven years she returns and goes to the king to petition for her house and land (wattēṣēʾ liszōq ʾel hammélek ʿal bêtāh wᵉʿal śādāh). The legal petition to the king for property restoration was the standard mechanism for resolving ownership disputes that arose during absence. In this case, she is seeking what she left behind when she obeyed the prophet's warning. Her obedience cost her seven years of land use; the question is whether the king's justice will restore it.
4: The king is speaking with Gehazi at this moment: wahammélek mᵉdabbēr ʾel Gēḥăzî naʿar ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm lēʾmōr sapĕrāh nāʾ lî ʾēt kol-haggᵉdōlôt ʾăšer ʿāśāh ʾĔlîšāʿ — tell me all the great things Elisha has done. The king's interest in Elisha's miracles at the exact moment the Shunammite woman enters creates a divinely timed confluence. The timing is not coincidental — YHWH's providential arrangement places her petition at the moment of maximum receptivity.
5: Gehazi is telling the story of the raised child: wayhî hûʾ mᵉsappēr lamélek ʾēt ʾăšer hehĕyyāh ʾet hammet wᵉhinnēh hāʾiššāh ʾăšer hehĕyyāh ʾet bᵉnāh ṣōʿaqat ʾel hammélek — and behold, the very woman whose son he raised comes crying to the king. The coincidence is a divine signature: the story being told and the storyteller of the story arrive simultaneously. hazzōʾt hāʾiššāh wᵉhazzeh bᵉnāh — this is the woman and this is her son. The living proof of the miracle is standing before the king as Gehazi testifies to it.
6: The king asks the woman, and she tells the whole story. He appoints an officer to restore her property: hāšēḇ ʾet kol-ʾăšer lāh wᵉʾēt kol-tᵉḇûʾôt haśśādeh mēyôm ʿāzᵉḇāh ʾet hāʾāreṣ wᵉʿad ʿattāh — restore everything that is hers, and all the produce of the field from the day she left until now. Full restitution including lost income — the king's justice is complete. The narrative demonstrates that obedience to prophetic warning is rewarded even years later with full restoration. The eschatological implication: those who sacrifice present possession for obedience to divine word receive full restitution (cf. Matthew 19:29).
7: Elisha goes to Damascus while Ben-hadad king of Aram is sick (wᵉBen-Hadad melek ʾărām ḥōleh). Hazael is sent to consult the prophet: leḵ qᵉrēʾ layyᵉhûdî ûdrôš ʾet YHWH môtô miḥŏlî zeh — go, inquire of the Israelite (YHWH) whether I will recover from this illness. The Aramean king seeking a Hebrew prophet to inquire of YHWH echoes the Naaman narrative (2 Kings 5): YHWH's prophetic authority extends beyond Israel's borders. The Elisha-Damascus-Hazael sequence will determine the fate of Syria, fulfilling Elijah's original commission to anoint Hazael (1 Kings 19:15).
8: Ben-hadad sends Hazael with a gift (wayyiqqaḥ minḥāh bᵉyādô) — forty camel-loads of all the good things of Damascus. The enormous gift-tribute reflects the Aramean king's desperation: he is dying and wants certainty about his fate. The minḥāh (gift, tribute) here is a diplomatic gift rather than a cultic offering — the gesture of a subordinate to a superior seeking information or favor. Elisha will receive the gift but give an answer the king did not expect.
9: Hazael comes and says: ʾāmarku bᵉnᵉḵā ʾăšer melek ʾărām Ben-Hadad ʾēlay ʾĕḥyeh mēḥŏlî zeh — your son (= vassal) Ben-hadad king of Aram has sent me to ask whether he will recover. ûḇinᵉḵā — “and your son” — the Aramean king addresses Elisha as “my father” by proxy, just as the king of Israel had done (2 Kings 6:21). The prophet's authority as spiritual father extends even to foreign kings.
10: Elisha says: lēḵ ʾĕmor lô ḥāyôh tiḥyeh wᵉhiggîd lî YHWH kî môt yāmût — go, say to him: “You will certainly recover” — but YHWH has shown me that he will certainly die. The apparent contradiction (will recover vs. will die) is resolved by Hazael's agency: Ben-hadad would recover from the illness, but he will be murdered by Hazael. Elisha knows not just the medical outcome but the political one. The prophetic word is not a simple prognosis but a complex disclosure of hidden intentions.
12: Hazael asks why Elisha weeps (cf. v11, present). Elisha answers: yādaʿtî ʾēt ʾăšer taʿăśeh liḇnê yiśrāʾēl rāʿāh miṣrôtêhem tᵉśallēaḥ bāʾēš wûḇḥurêhem tahărog baḥereḇ wᵉʿōlᵉlêhem tᵉrattēš wᵉhārōtêhem tᵉḇaqqēaʿ — I know the evil you will do to Israel: their strongholds you will set on fire, their young men you will kill with the sword, their infants you will dash in pieces, their pregnant women you will rip open. Elisha weeps because he sees the violence before it happens — the prophetic burden of seeing coming judgment while being unable to prevent it. Jesus weeps over Jerusalem for the same reason (Luke 19:41-44), knowing the destruction of 70 CE.
13: Hazael protests: kî mah ʿaḇdᵉḵā hakkeléḇ hammēt kî yaʿăśeh haddāḇār haggādôl hazzeh — what is your servant, a dead dog, that he should do this great thing? The self-deprecation is formally humble but structurally revealing: Hazael denies the capacity for such atrocity while already calculating it. Elisha responds: hirrʾānî YHWH ʾōtᵉḵā melek ʾărām — YHWH has shown me you will be king of Aram. The two prophecies are connected: the power to do these things requires kingship, and kingship is already prophetically assigned.
14: Hazael returns to Ben-hadad who asks: mah ʾāmar lᵉḵā ʾĔlîšāʿ — what did Elisha say? Hazael lies: wayyōʾmer lî ḥāyôh tiḥyeh — he told me you would certainly live. The lie preserves Hazael's position while enabling the murder. Hazael has just received a prophetic commission to greatness; he implements it through deception and violence — a pattern of human agency actualizing prophetic word through morally compromised means. The sovereignty of YHWH's decree and the responsibility of human sin coexist without the narrative resolving the tension.
15: The next day Hazael takes a thick cloth (hammakbēr), dips it in water, and spreads it over Ben-hadad's face until he dies. wayyimloḵ Ḥăzāʾēl taḥtāyw — Hazael reigns in his place. The murder fulfills both Elisha's prophecy (Hazael becomes king) and Elijah's original commission (1 Kings 19:15: wᵉʾet ḥăzāʾēl timšaḥ lᵉmelek ʿal ʾărām). The kingship is bestowed through prophetic commission but actualized through regicide — YHWH's purposes are accomplished through violent human choices, which does not exonerate the human actor.
16: The Judean synchronism: Jehoram son of Jehoshaphat begins to reign over Judah in Joram son of Ahab's fifth year. Jehoshaphat was Judah's most faithful king after David and Solomon; his son Jehoram is its most faithless. The DH explains: wayyēlek bᵉdereḵ malkê yiśrāʾēl — he walked in the way of the kings of Israel. The marriage alliance with Ahab's house (v18) introduces northern-style Baalism into Judah. The pattern of the faithful parent followed by the apostate child repeats throughout Kings.
17: Jehoram of Judah's eight-year reign is noted. His age at accession (thirty-two) and eight-year reign are given without any positive evaluation — a stark contrast to the evaluations of most Judean kings. The absence of positive characterization in the regnal formula is itself a form of condemnation in the DH's evaluative framework.
18: wayyēlek bᵉdereḵ malkê yiśrāʾēl kaʾăšer ʿāśû bêt ʾAḥāḇ kî bat ʾAḥāḇ hāytāh lô lᵉʾiššāh — he walked in the way of the kings of Israel as the house of Ahab had done, for the daughter of Ahab was his wife. The marriage alliance between Jehoshaphat's heir and Ahab's daughter (Athaliah) introduces the Jezebel pattern into Jerusalem. The DH traces religious corruption through marriage alliances — Solomon's wives, Ahab's Jezebel, now Athaliah — consistent with Deuteronomy's warning against intermarriage with idolatrous peoples (Deuteronomy 7:3-4).
19: wᵉlōʾ ʾābāh YHWH lᵉhašḥît ʾet yᵉhûdāh lᵉmaʿan dāwid ʿaḇdô kaʾăšer ʾāmar lô lātēt lô nîr lᵉḇānāyw kol hayyāmîm — yet YHWH was not willing to destroy Judah for the sake of David his servant, as he promised to give him a lamp (nîr) for his sons always. The nîr (lamp, perpetual fire) for the Davidic dynasty is the covenant of 2 Sam 7 expressed as the preservation of the line despite apostasy. Judah's survival through idolatrous reigns is not merit-based but covenant-based — the unconditional Davidic promise. The NT reads this preservation as pointing to the ultimate Davidic heir in whom the covenant is finally and fully actualized (Luke 1:32-33; Revelation 5:5).
20: During Jehoram's reign, Edom revolts from Judah's authority and installs its own king (wayyimloḵ ʿălêhem melek). The Edom revolt reverses the subjugation established under David (2 Samuel 8:14). The loss of Edomite territory is a visible symptom of covenant infidelity: YHWH promised expansion and security for faithfulness; defections and revolts signal the withdrawal of that protection. Edom's eventual salvation is promised in the prophets (Amos 9:12 in its NT application via Acts 15:16-17) as part of the eschatological restoration of all nations.
21: Jehoram goes to Zair and strikes the Edomites who surround him — he breaks through at night (wayyaqqom laylāh wayyaḵ ʾet ʾĕdôm hasSōḇēḇ ʾēlāyw). The campaign is inconclusive: the army flees home (wayyānos hāʿām lᵉʾōhālāyw). A military breakout from encirclement is presented without clear victory — Jehoram escapes but does not reconquer. The political weakness is theological: apostate kings do not receive the full covenant-warrior protection.
22: wayyipšaʿ ʾĕdôm mittaḥat yad yᵉhûdāh ʿad hayyôm hazzeh — Edom revolted from the hand of Judah to this day. The narrator's “to this day” formula marks permanent political loss. The Libnah revolt mentioned in the same verse represents a second defection within the land — even a Judean city repudiates Jehoram's authority. The combined losses signal a kingdom contracting under divine judgment.
23: The closing formula for Jehoram of Judah: wᵉyeter diḇrê Yᵉhôrām wᵉḵol ʾăšer ʿāśāh hălōʾ hēm kᵉtûḇîm ʿal sēper diḇrê hayyāmîm lᵉmalkê yᵉhûdāh — the rest of Jehoram's acts are written in the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah. The source citation signals the court archive; Jehoram's full military and administrative record was available there.
24: wayyiškaḇ Yᵉhôrām ʿim ʾăḇōtāyw wayyiqqāḇēr ʾim ʾăḇōtāyw bᵉʿîr dāwid — Jehoram slept with his fathers and was buried with his fathers in the city of David. Ahaziah his son reigned in his place. The burial in the city of David continues even for apostate Judean kings — the Davidic city still receives its line. The burial is honorable in form but the narrative has made clear the king's apostasy.
25: Ahaziah son of Jehoram begins to reign over Judah — the synchronism is the twelfth year of Joram son of Ahab of Israel. Ahaziah's one-year reign makes him one of the shortest-lived Judean kings. His entire significance in the DH is as the fulcrum of the Jehu revolution that will destroy the house of Ahab and nearly extinguish the Davidic line (via Athaliah's usurpation in 2 Kings 11).
26: Ahaziah of Judah's mother is Athaliah, daughter of Omri — ûšem ʾimmô ʿĂṯalyāhû bat ʿOmrî melek yiśrāʾēl. Athaliah is both the daughter of Omri (or granddaughter — 2 Chronicles 22:2 says daughter of Ahab) and the wife of Jehoram of Judah. The Omride connection runs through the queen mother into the Judean royal house. Athaliah's later seizure of the Davidic throne (2 Kings 11) makes this genealogical connection the pivot of Judah's near-extinction.
27: wayyēlek bᵉdereḵ bêt ʾAḥāḇ wayyaʿaś hāraʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH kᵉbêt ʾAḥāḇ kî ḥōtēn bêt ʾAḥāḇ hûʾ — he walked in the way of the house of Ahab and did evil in YHWH's eyes, as the house of Ahab had done, for he was a son-in-law (ḥōtēn) of the house of Ahab. The marriage alliance is the vehicle of theological corruption: Ahab's religious practices enter Judah through family connection. The DH's insistence that Ahaziah's evil is specifically modeled on Ahab's house creates a deliberate intertextual link between the parallel northern and southern apostasies.
28: Ahaziah goes with Joram son of Ahab to war against Hazael of Aram at Ramoth-gilead — the same front where Ahab died (1 Kgs 22). The Ramoth-gilead connection is deliberate: the city where Ahab met prophetically-announced death is now the site where the Ahab-dynasty connection begins its unraveling. Joram is wounded (wayyakkû ʾărammîm ʾet Yôrām) and returns to Jezreel to recover.
29: Ahaziah goes to Jezreel to visit Joram who was recovering from wounds received at Ramah. wᵉʾăḥazyāhû ben Yᵉhôrām melek yᵉhûdāh yārad lirʾôt ʾet Yôrām ben ʾAḥāḇ bîzrᵉʿel kî ḥōleh hûʾ. The visit to the recuperating king places Ahaziah at Jezreel at the moment Jehu's coup will begin (2 Kings 9-10) — the geography of the narrative is tightening around the coming judgment on the house of Ahab and its Judean ally. The convergence of both kings at Jezreel will be the setting for both their deaths.
